pick.rationale: This is a classic 8/9 coin-flip game, but Ohio State holds a slight edge as reflected by the 2.5-point spread. The Buckeyes boast a stronger offense (ORtg 118.6, 35th nationally) and played a significantly tougher schedule in the Big Ten (SOS 12.06, 16th nationally; SRS 19.06, 27th), which gives their 21-12 record more weight than TCU's 22-11 mark. While TCU's elite defense (KenPom AdjD 97.8, Torvik AdjDE 30th) is a legitimate equalizer, Ohio State's battle-tested roster—now healthier heading into March after dealing with injuries to key players like Brandon Noel, Devin Royal, and Christoph Tilly throughout the season—and their offensive firepower led by Bruce Thornton give them the narrow advantage in what projects as a tight, low-margin contest.

result.text: Ohio State's seed, opponent, location for NCAA Basketball Tournament Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Recruiting Big Ten Sports Odds BuckeyesXtra Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Ohio State's seed, opponent, location for NCAA Basketball Tournament Yesh Ginsburg Buckeyes Wire The Ohio State Buckeyes learned their NCAA Tournament fate very early in the selection process on Sunday, being just the third team to have their name called during the selection show. The Buckeyes were selected as the No. 8 seed in the East Region of the bracket, with No. 9 TCU selected as their opening opponent. The Buckeyes are in the same "pod" of the tournament as No. 1 overall seed Duke, meaning that the Buckeyes almost certainly have a meeting with the Blue Devils to look forward to if they can win their opening game. TCU went 22-11 on their season, but closed out the season on an impressive run. Then again, so did Ohio State. Buckeyes fans have a good game to look forward to against a quality opponent, with a battle with the best team in the country the likely reward for winning. This pod will play on Thursday in Greenville, South Carolina. Tip-off times will be announced after the completion of the entire selection show. On Jan. 2, the Buckeyes resumed Big Ten play with an 80-73 road win against Rutgers. Sixth-year Wright State transfer Brandon Noel came off the bench to play 20 minutes in relief of freshman Amare Bynum, who teamed with Devin Royal, John Mobley Jr., Christoph Tilly and, of course, ever-reliable Bruce Thornton in the starting lineup. Three days later, Noel went down with a fracture in his left foot during a home loss to No. 10 Nebraska. He’s yet to return to action. Since that date, injuries and sickness have sidelined Tilly, Royal and Mobley at various points, sometimes forcing coach Jake Diebler to make last-minute lineup adjustments. The end of the season is on the horizon, but so is the potential of a healthy roster. With four games left before the Big Ten Tournament and the Buckeyes still with work to do to make the NCAA Tournament, Diebler said he increasingly believes Ohio State will dress a full roster before the postseason begins. “I’m hopeful, prayerful that we’re going to turn the corner on some of this stuff here really, really soon,” he said. “And if we do that, and get everybody available and moving in the same direction, watch out. I really believe that.” More immediately, that would mean the returns of both Mobley and Royal to the lineup. The former has missed three straight games with what Diebler confirmed is a finger injury suffered in a home win against USC on Feb. 11. The injury required a surgery, and Mobley was nearly cleared to play at No. 15 Michigan State on Feb. 22 but instead had to watch from the bench. Diebler said he’s “getting better and better each day” as the Buckeyes prepare to play at Iowa on Feb. 25. “With this injury there was never this specific date, there was this threshold that has to be met,” he said. “It’s a day-to-day thing for him, but there’s been progress. He is working so hard to get back as quickly as he can.” Royal joined Mobley and Noel on the bench inside the Breslin Center after coming down with an illness that had kept Bynum out of practice the day before the game. result.text: Could Ohio State basketball get back to full health before postseason? Skip to main content Home USMNT USWNT MLS NWSL Newsletter Americans Abroad Concacaf Champions Cup Premier League Champions League Mexico USA TODAY Sports As Ohio State gets healthier, coach Jake Diebler hoping for late push Adam Jardy Columbus Dispatch It’s been nearly two months since the Ohio State men’s basketball team started and finished a game with all its primary rotation players available. On Jan. 2, the Buckeyes resumed Big Ten play with an 80-73 road win against Rutgers. Sixth-year Wright State transfer Brandon Noel came off the bench to play 20 minutes in relief of freshman Amare Bynum, who teamed with Devin Royal, John Mobley Jr., Christoph Tilly and, of course, ever-reliable Bruce Thornton in the starting lineup. Three days later, Noel went down with a fracture in his left foot during a home loss to No. 10 Nebraska. He’s yet to return to action. Since that date, injuries and sickness have sidelined Tilly, Royal and Mobley at various points, sometimes forcing coach Jake Diebler to make last-minute lineup adjustments. The end of the season is on the horizon, but so is the potential of a healthy roster. With four games left before the Big Ten Tournament and the Buckeyes still with work to do to make the NCAA Tournament, Diebler said he increasingly believes Ohio State will dress a full roster before the postseason begins. “I’m hopeful, prayerful that we’re going to turn the corner on some of this stuff here really, really soon,” he said. “And if we do that, and get everybody available and moving in the same direction, watch out. I really believe that.” More immediately, that would mean the returns of both Mobley and Royal to the lineup. The former has missed three straight games with what Diebler confirmed is a finger injury suffered in a home win against USC on Feb. 11. The injury required a surgery, and Mobley was nearly cleared to play at No. 15 Michigan State on Feb. 22 but instead had to watch from the bench. Diebler said he’s “getting better and better each day” as the Buckeyes prepare to play at Iowa on Feb. 25. “With this injury there was never this specific date, there was this threshold that has to be met,” he said. “It’s a day-to-day thing for him, but there’s been progress. He is working so hard to get back as quickly as he can.” Royal joined Mobley and Noel on the bench inside the Breslin Center after coming down with an illness that had kept Bynum out of practice the day before the game. The freshman was able to battle through it and start against the Spartans, but Royal did not play after looking labored while going through warmups.
